item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i love how the note at the end just seems exasperated. like, "for fuck's sake, it's a goddamn meme, use the safety procedures you morons".

item.title: ...Cause you've Got a Squid in Me...
item.content:encoded:
Cause you've Got a Squid in Me
I like you
Cause u got that i need
In my life
So give it to me
Cause you've Got a Squid in Me…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something that someone said above. Just think about what's going on in 54 now that it has been quarantined… My guess is some kind of Cephalopod/Sarkic wet dream and that made me shiver.

Upvoted with my ugly and calloused skin.
I think this squid might have an out-of-universe effect on people to make it so highly upvoted — personally I've started coming around to it myself, or at the very least disliking it less.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A wild blend of multiple anomalous attributes surrounding a certain cuddly cephalopod
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is cute/gross, but the idea isn't really grabbing me. It's good, but it's not something that I actually like. Novote

item.title: Fanart!
item.content:encoded: Check out this comic about 2790!
You maniacs! You blew it up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The new picture that was just added is much much better in my opinion. Looks much squishier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
resulted in increased containment breach rates from 0 per week to 0 per day.

only 87% of test subjects had suffered complications from the grafting procedures

These are my favorite parts. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even SCPs are not immune to Rule 34.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well I went on Tumblr and there is porn of this now, what did I even expect of life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This thing is so sinisterly adorable. What a squishable little monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just realized that the self-contradictory language, particularly "only 87%", reminded me of the doublethink pervading the Paranoia RPG, which is a good thing from my perspective. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: its so cute! im willing to be its friend?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2790 must be loved with lots of care. Poke him and prod him and hug him and squeeze him and rub against him and play with him but don't touch him. Personnel that touch 2790 will be severely punished.

Not many articles get me to upvote before I'm done reading the containment procedures. +1

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: I'd like to thank my parents, the Academy, and everyone who told me to delete this when I first showed it around

That was… odd.

But hey, I liked it. Congrats on the +100 article ARD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed the typo (I don't make arithmetic mistakes)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anomalous math.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
189 personnel have volunteered for grafting trials. Although 72 had to be rejected for health reasons, 127 personnel were selected

Hmm…
American Foundation uses peculiar math, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, this was posted before I left? How did I miss it? ARD, take your +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Squids have blood; it's just that their blood, like that of many invertebrates, contains hemocyanin (a copper-based oxygen transport molecule) instead of hemoglobin. This is because at the temperature and depth at which many of these invertebrates live, hemocyanin is more efficient than hemoglobin.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just so you know, it's not made of glass. It's made of flesh and blood (or whatever circulatory fluid squid have instead of blood). "Glass" is part of the species name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, thanks. I didn't really get that until I read your comment.

Overall, a nice SCP. I feel as if it's very simple though (kind of a one-trick pony). If it were much longer I feel it would have dragged on the idea for too long and become boring. Luckily it went on for just the right length. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me at "Object Class: BFF"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: something more dangerous: grafting is completed and works on 3-6 persons. they spread the love. (effect)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creepy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed reading this one, and your subtle choice of wording ("only 87%", for example) simply and effectively pushed just how far this thing had gotten into their minds. Also, picturing what's been going on in Site-54 since being isolated is just a horrible, horrible thought. Well done; plus one!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It reads like a -J, but the implications are worthy of a real SCP number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I saw the image caption and object class and did a double-take. Where's the -J?

Turns out, people can still write creepy shit on this site, and I love this, particularly the mantra against touching him. You can just feel the document writer's mind breaking as they try desperately to stave off this memetic influence, to warn others that the site's been compromised. This is really great story-telling, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get what it's trying to do, but the cutesy theme just makes it a pain to read. It reads too similar to a cuted out -J, which never worked for me. It did get better on the addendum, where the more interesting effects of the SCP were shown, but wasn't good enough to compensate for the tone of the article. Nothing inherently bad in the article, but rather a preference in format screws.

His breach rate has also decreased from 0 breaches per day to 0.

This made me chuckle though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The way this is laid out hurts my suspension of disbelief. Most format screws of this type tend to have the explanation for why it is the way it is at the beginning, because that helps with the impression that this is a professional document with an actual purpose. This feels more like a document that might be attached to the main report in order to illustrate the things effects.

I get that having the note about why the format screw has been preserved at the end is supposed to allow the wrongness to draw the reader in, but it had the opposite effect on me. I had to force myself to get through the article to find the note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the issue is that the site is quarintined, and they may have disabled the failsafe due to bring enthralled by the SCP.

The Foundation can't remotely terminate the SCP, and if all research data on the SCP is at the site, you have a very distinct possibility that any extermination teams you send in get trapped by the lotus-eater machine going on in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ultra coincidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of my own SCP-2600-CU.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When we're going for something that not only is hazardous enough to infect a whole site, but does infect a whole site, it better be at a level of OTT enough to do so. SCP-2690 does this via religious means (though I wasn't much of a fan otherwise), and 1561 piled it on as the article went on.

The goofy lovey-dovey tone doesn't do it favors, because, and maybe it's me, once we see something so saccharine and sweet, our indicators go off and we sense something is fucked. In addition, we get the idea very soon that oh hey, they grafted its skin onto theirs. This isn't subtle, but tries to present it as gradual and subtle. And that does no favors for me, because this would be so much better if it were going the extra steps to make this OTT.

Also, as a final note, there's no reason to not neutralize it. Say what you want about the Foundation; this is a large-scale containment breach, and it doesn't take much for this to potentially spill out into something far more dangerous. 2690 uses the religious motivators as a means to not neutralize this, and 1561… well, they want to, but can't. But there's no other reasons here. And any excuse for the Foundation to not be aware of it is dashed by the note at the end (and nothing to indicate they took care of the problem), which breaks my disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I second the "feeling opposite" thing. The big appeal here is the subtle, simple effect as opposed to SCP-1561's blunt (though well done) play of the "thing that makes people obey you" trope. This goes from something very easy to write off as harmless and silly to something that could plausibly infect and destroy an entire site because it seems harmless and silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm the opposite of this, cute glass squid is actually a lot creepier to me than psychic compelling crown. I like people replacing their skin with glass a lot more than people randomly worshipping a crown dude and taking over the Foundation. At least the latter makes intuitive sense. This doesn't, and in a good way. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of SCP-1561 (an infohazard ruining an entire Site), but doesn't take it as crazy or as far as it did, thus underwhelming. Yes, it's creepy and disgusting, and the language here definitely hammers it in. But it doesn't ever feel over-the-top or ridiculous, and since we already have an example of this kind of thing done well and done in a way to hammer it in repeatedly, I can't help but think that this lacks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So you are directed to pet, prod, poke and hug it but also never to touch it? What do people who are exposed actually do?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. The style is so fun, and it works so well as a sort of internal cautionary document. I think it might be good to have the note at the end, even if it doesn't make perfect sense in universe. The breakdown throughout the article of logic is just too good, and it might reinforce that a bit to not know this is something that's been handled already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the idea is that it originally said not to touch it because that's how it affects people, but then the effect caused them to think something along the lines of 'well, rubbing isn't touching, it's a different word'. Cognitostuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://dictionary.reference.com/browse/touch

In all seriousness, that's part of the effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't poorly written, but it's not much different from the ton of cogito/memetic/infohazardous SCPs that fuck with the people writing their documentation. Hell, there was one for the contest that involved worshipping a dead angel, with the site lockdown and everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Obviously I am really missing something but what does "touch" actually mean here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting it primarily for the deviations from the standard SCP format. While nothing really original (due to all the format screw SCPs), this one was mildly funny for me.

item.title: Hmm
item.content:encoded:
Let's see how this one goes. This was a tricky one to write. I tried making other changes to it past this point, but those left me unsatisfied. This one was originally an idea for the Short Story Contest that was shelved in favor of SCP-2810 and revived. Thanks to Cavalry, Cyclopian, Drazool, DreamwalkerFae, polearms, psul, rumetzen, and probably others for feedback.

Also grudging thanks to Decibelles, Lex1nat0r, and TwistedGears for telling me to fundamentally alter the original concept of this one (guess what tho I didn't delete it so eat it).

See more of my work on my author page!

Image sourcing for SQUID.JPG:
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Teuthowenia_megalops_(Michael_Vecchione,_NOAA).jpg -
Closeup of the eyes of Teuthowenia megalops (ventral view). The three photophores are visible on each eye (light yellow-golden areas). Michael Vecchione, NOAA Fisheries Service.
This image is in the public domain because it contains materials that originally came from the U.S. National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration, taken or made as part of an employee's official duties.
